sections.paragraph: an unused root, which I regard as onomatopoetic, and kindred to the verb כָּנַר (as to the connection of the letter צ with the palatals, see last remark under , p. 698, A ); pr. to creak, to squeak, Germ. fchnarren, fchnurren (like כָּנַר knarren); specially used of the stridulous sound of water flowing down violently, as in cataracts, aqueducts (raufchen). Hence צִנֹּור. The same power appears to have belonged to כָּנַר and the quadriliterals צַנְתַּר, גַּנְסַר; whence יַם־כִּנֶּרֶת and יָם־גְּנֵיסַר the sea of Galilee, perhaps the sea of the cataracts (from the Jordan flowing into this lake with a great rushing), and צַנְתָּר cantharus, i.e. a channel.
